item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find this SCP of particular interest in that it could potentially have serious implications for the foundation itself. Think about it. If it is a library that contains possibly every book that could ever exist, then it would stand to reason that somewhere inside the library lies the following:

Every possible SCP that ever exists.
Every complete explaination for every SCP that exists.
Every complete explaination for every SCP written in such a way that even the average layman can understand it perfectly.
The answer to what SCP-001 really is.
Which SCPs need to be contained and which ones can be safely released without any ill effects.
The complete history of the foundation to it's very beginnings.
And more.

But conversely while the truth behind every SCP can be found in 1986, so can every lie as well.

It is unbearable knowledge that somewhere in this place, the answers to all of the foundation's questions exist yet remains inaccessible. If only there was a way to determine which books would lead to these truths, we could finally provide humanity with some sense of security and control when exposed to these anomalies since they would be anomalies no more. But as far as we know, no such thing exists.

Such pondering could drive one to utter insanity.

item.title: Latin title
item.content:encoded: On the assumption that the title of the last example translates as ‘On the existence and divinity of God’, the grammatical Latin would be ‘De existentia et divinitate Dei’.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not on this one. You can tell when anyone is allowed to add because one of the tags at the bottom will be "collaborative" - this article doesn't have it. Thanks for checking first!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this one of those open pages where anyone can add books, or is only the poster of the SCP allowed to?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Title: The Need for Legalized Abortion
Author: anonymous
Language: English, modern
Depth of recovery: 28,111m
Description: A polemic on the moral, medical, and societal costs of illegal abortion.

Me: eh. Pretty odd, given it's depth

Makes the case that abortion should be permitted through the fourth trimester.

Excuse me what the fuck

item.title: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
https://www.pexels.com/photo/book-books-circle-curly-87007/

Image Source, Public Domain

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not really sure what the protocol is for replying to very old posts, but I have a couple of answers.

The restrictions in the last note (only 24 possible characters, only 410 pages per book, etc.) only apply to the Library of Babel from the short story by Borges, not to SCP-1986. I can't say for sure, but I'd assume the author included the Borges quote mostly as an acknowledgement of inspiration—and to cheekily imply that in the Foundation-verse, Borges' own story was inspired by an encounter with this scip.

And finally, if there were actually no order to which these texts were organized, the chances of finding anything recognizable as English would be literally zero, mathematically speaking, as there are an infinite amount of intelligible texts and a much smaller infinity of intelligible ones. And then to say that some texts were found that are actual novels present in the real world, that's even more impossible. So there has to be some kind of organization to the texts.

That is, in fact, the whole point Borges' story: In a library with every possible book, lacking clear organization or a reliable search algorithm, it would be impossible to find any useful knowledge. Too much information is just as unhelpful as a lack of information.

But that's another point where Borges' Library of Babel differs from SCP-1986. Borges' Library is the totality of every possible book that could be written, given certain restrictions: every possible unique result from a random text generator. So you get some infinitesimal islands of sense in an unimaginably vast ocean of gibberish. On the other hand, SCP-1986 appears to contain every book that has been written across the entirety of the multiverse, so: "The works range from being somewhat unusual to completely nonsensical, though nominally at least intelligible."

Which makes a much more entertaining setting for a pop-scifi work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I used to search Library of Babel, 2 years ago.!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems Schultz wasn't dying of a head wound, according to Wikipedia, but instead was shot in the body and sustained internal damage. He spent a lot of time talking mostly incoherently and eventually died of peritonitis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Headcanoning this as a section of the Wanderer's library that intersects with our world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just going to add this to my list of "Libraries I'd sell organs to visit"

Right up there with:
The Library Planet from Doctor Who.
The Library from The Librarian.
The Library of Congress.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"the Moon landing in 1969 was in fact a hoax perpetrated on a sound-stage located on the surface of the Moon."

That is one of my favorite parts, and it's funny as I'll get out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for nice explanation.
You are sooo kind!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A little more about Running Bird Soosaaw'kway:
Translation of Native American names can be complicated, but it this case it seems that Running Bird is the personal name, (which is typically translated because the meaning is the important part) while the second part seems to be a family name of some sort with no translation.
So something like

Running Bird("달리기 새") Soosaaw'kway

might be close to what you want, after accounting for my limited understanding of Hangeul.
You probably could phonetically translate "Soosaaw'kway", but I'll leave that to people not relying on the combination of a kindergarten-level understanding of Hanzi-group languages and google translate. X3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Running Bird Soosaaw'kway

It is supposed to be the name of a fictional (I think) Ojibwe poet. The given name is in English and the surname is presumably Ojibwe (I have no idea if that is a real Ojibwe name or not though).

gustofacial response

The behavior of infants in which tasting different foods produces fairly predictable facial expressions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello. I'm Korean SCP user, translating SCP-1986 to Korean. Sorry to poor English X)

I have a little question. What this mean? :
1. running bird Soosaaw'kway
2. Gusto-Facial Response

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An English-Cetacean Phrasebook

Upvoted, as dolphins are my favorite animal.

Winnie the Pooh

Let me upvoted again XD

the Moon landing in 1969 was in fact a hoax perpetrated on a sound-stage located on the surface of the Moon.

Whhhhhhyyyyy can't I upvoted more Wikidot!?!?!?!😧

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minor nitpick. In the description of Charm for the protection of a child, the sentence "Much of the material remains a matter of conjecture as much of it resists translation." is a bit awkwardly worded in its repetition of the word "much of". It could perhaps be reworded so as to either replace one of the "much of"s with a synonym, or to remove one entirely.
Otherwise, I love this one, and am in the process of thinking up potential log additions to run by you.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Young Lady's Guide to Etiquette and Grooming is my favorite.

"Included are illustrations of the correct placement of the tail for various social settings, how a lady may best scale a tree and preserve her modesty, and the best way to fend off unwanted advances while in estrus."

Place your tails right, Ladies!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not really sure what the protocol is for commenting on very old posts, but I have a couple of nitpicks with the last note there. For one, "the space, the period, the comma, the twenty-two letters of the alphabet." There are quite a few other punctuation marks used very commonly in basically all literature, unless the library is just removing those. There are also 26 characters in the Roman alphabet.

Next is this, " its shelves register all the possible combinations of the twenty-odd orthographical symbols (a number which, though extremely vast, is not infinite)" So either you're defining a limit to how long these texts can be, which isn't referenced anywhere in the article, or the number of permutations is infinite. If it's every combination of letters it has to be infinite, the books would just escalate in length.

And finally, if there were actually no order to which these texts were organized, the chances of finding anything recognizable as English would be literally zero, mathematically speaking, as there are an infinite amount of intelligible texts and a much smaller infinity of intelligible ones. And then to say that some texts were found that are actual novels present in the real world, that's even more impossible. So there has to be some kind of organization to the texts.

If any of these points have already been made, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ooooh. A nonsense Library in an infinite tunnel in Buenos Aires. What's not to like?

I didn't read all the comments, so you might already have answered this: where did you find that fascinating picture? (just point me to the discussion page… please…. pretty please…?)

This shoots right up to one of my top favouritest SCPs in the known universe. +12 to the nth power where n > 957 842 156 487 596

*off to explore the new idea discussion*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I was lost and amused, then I reached the actual recovered books and upvoted.

However, as a Dutch guy, I feel compelled to tell you that the title of "Grote Werken en Human Leugen (trans: Great Work in Human Fiction)" makes no sense in Dutch. I can give a translation, but I wanted to make sure it wasn't your intention first :P

Great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I -do- still accept submissions….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…the Moon landing in 1969 was in fact a hoax perpetrated on a sound-stage located on the surface of the Moon.

aaaand upvoted. Not that I wasn't going to once I'd realized the scale (realizing 4,400k is as far as WE'VE gotten, and we haven't seen the end yet)

Dr. Mann, I diagree with you. That footnote is the only reason I had a clue. Much wiki-ing was required from there to understand completely. My only issue is it's too long to be read from the insert point at 100%, you have to jump to the note itself to read the entirety.

I adore this. Am thinking up ideas for submission (if you're still taking them). All my +1.

P.S. Still wondering how I've missed this for as long as I've been here. Hidden diamond!!

Edit: Squee! I have posted a pair to the submission thread, hope you like them!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i posted my own idea of a book at http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-566999/opening-of-the-imaginary-library-scp-1986-to-new-ideas#post-2200829

i dont know if it is still active, but by god, this is a good concept!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just read this one (I'm working on a somewhat similar article, and was cautioned to keep it somewhat different), and I like it. However, I think the discovery log would be better without the footnote. Readers who care about Borges are going to make the connection on their own, and it would be much more satisfying for them than having it spelled out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So this is what heaven looks like…

item.title: Favourite SCP
item.content:encoded: Long-time lurker recently joined, just wanted to say this is my favourite SCP. Upvoted!

item.title: Duplicated word
item.content:encoded:
Fixing a minor error

Title: The Annotated Diaries of Woodrow Wilson, Volume 2
Author: Edited by Dr. T.K.L. Conyer
Language: English, Modern
Depth of recovery: 1,331m
Description: The second of an unknown number of volumes of the private diaries of U.S. President Woodrow Wilson, detailing the political battle for Thailand's statehood, his thoughts on the the 20th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution (granting citizenship to "The Afflicted"), and his personal doubts about being named Presbyter of the Imperial States of America.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great detail in every one of these books. Now I'm kind of annoyed that I can never read them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you look at the first revision of Doctor GE Smith's message, you can see the string of +1s is breaking the site formatting and makes a bottom scroll bar necessary even on widescreen monitors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What does scantron's quote mean?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Snip. Was stretching the screen.- Scantron

[Edit:Oops, sorry! To preserve intent, there was a long sting of +1+1+etc. I didn't consider formatting.]

meh… it's OK, I guess…

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article has such a vivid mental image to me. It's just so creative. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would have upvoted for The Grasshopper Lies Heavy alone. As it is, I found at least twelve pressing reasons to upvote. And it's all your own work, too. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thanks very much

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I swear I'm in love with this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Personnel are reminded that SCP-1986 is situated within the grounds of a public library, and are thus urged to use their 'indoor voices'."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All I can contribute at this point is that the Librarians must have a wicked awesome electric hovercraft to safely and speedily traverse over 400k down a tunnel paved with books. And now I am picturing the Foundation's librarians hooting and hollering, all of them dressed like 'Conan: the Librarian' (from Weird Al's movie UHF), log hair whipping in the breeze. Energized by the thought of their conquest of yet another deep book recovery mission.

item.title: Re: +200
item.content:encoded: thanks Scantron. Who knew?

item.title: Re: +200
item.content:encoded: I love this one!
books are for nerds

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: heh heh heh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A novel concerning an alternate world where the United States won WWII (but not in a manner consistent with our history)

I know this article is ancient but is this SCP set in an alternate universe to our own? I'm not aware of any manner consistent with our history where the United States won WWII.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hhhehehe Omg it's been three h's the entire time. My life is a lie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hehe

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it bad that I really want to read Cannibalism and the Free Market?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Up-voting: It may not be creepy but the idea of a library like this intrigues me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thanks buddy bill…contribute your own!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no IDEA what I just read, but I love it. I admit that I laughed at the "lady etiquette and grooming" thing (a society of furry folk, obviously, and given tree-climbing, catgirls jump to mind), and at the "Noble Prize" thing, and at… oh hell I laughed at everything, it was great. (I find confusion humorous)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Borges's library, huh? That explains a lot, actually…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As an MLS student, I both have to love and fear this SCP. A couple of my favorites:

the Moon landing in 1969 was in fact a hoax fabricated on a sound-stage located on the surface of the Moon.

This just came out of nowhere and made me cackle like an idiot.

A cookbook describing the proper preparation and cooking of words and phrases in a wide variety of meals.

Reminded me so much of "The Phantom Tollbooth", one of my favorite books when I was a kid.

Upvoted, and I hope to see more 'finds'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks…I thought it was funny too.
My favorite though is "The Nobel prize for Obstinance"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Adult-Onset Sudden Infant Death Syndrome…brilliant. Multiple +1's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Library is Accepting Submissions
For those interested, go over to this thread and suggest possible inclusions to a curated list of notable Recovered Volumes

http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-566999/opening-of-the-imaginary-library-scp-1986-to-new-ideas

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
go over to this thread and contribute a suggested volume!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can I just say that this is one of my favorite articles on this site? I would upvote it again if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're really funny Gene =D

…and I see you contributed one yourself, namely Buenos Aires!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I went with Declan McManus (Elvis Costello's birth name) because its more obscure…and I like EC better :)
